chemicals.description: Considered non-toxic, but ingestion of leaves may cause mild gastrointestinal upset.
chemicals.name: Non-toxic / Mild Irritant

description: Spider Plant (Chlorophytum spp.) is generally considered safe for cats and is not listed as toxic by the ASPCA. However, spider plants contain compounds with mild hallucinogenic properties related to opium (similar in structure but much weaker than true opioids). This can cause cats to act intoxicated and nibble the plant repeatedly.
name: Spider Plant
safetyNotes: Classified as non-toxic, but contains mild hallucinogenic compounds that may attract cats
Symptoms if eaten excessively: mild GI upset (vomiting, diarrhea), apparent intoxication or unusual behaviour
The mild attraction effect is why some cats repeatedly chew spider plants despite GI upset
Not a medical emergency; limit access if your cat obsessively chews the plant
No veterinary treatment required for typical small-amount nibbling

symptoms.name: Mild nausea or digestive upset (which may include vomiting)
symptoms.notes: This is a rare and mild physical reaction to consuming heavy amounts of fibrous plant material, not a toxicological reaction6. No veterinary action is needed unless the digestive upset is prolonged or the cat begins to show other unusual behaviors

treatments.description: The owner simply monitors the cat for minor digestive upset or mild nausea after the cat consumes the plant material
treatments.name: At-Home Observation
treatments.notes: Because there are no poisonous compounds in the plant, no medical action is needed unless the cat exhibits unusual behavior or the mild digestive upset becomes prolonged2. 2. Symptomatic Veterinary Care (Only if Upset is Prolonged)

treatments.description: If the cat's digestive upset does not resolve on its own, a veterinarian may examine the cat and provide basic supportive care for an upset stomach.
treatments.name: Symptomatic Gastrointestinal Support
treatments.notes: This is very rarely necessary. It would only be indicated if the cat ate such a massive amount of the non-toxic plant material that it caused an unusual or persistent physical stomach ache, rather than a toxicological reaction
